summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an unauthenticated client can connect to the Oxia data server's internal gRPC port and send a crafted SendSnapshot RPC whose SnapshotChunk.Name contains path traversal sequences, causing the server to create a file outside the database directory at an arbitrary filesystem path.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause the data server to create a proof file at /tmp/evohunt-proof containing the string EVOHUNT-PATH-TRAVERSAL-PROOF. This file resides outside the configured database directory, demonstrating that path traversal in SnapshotChunk.Name allows arbitrary file write.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the Oxia data server from the target repository, started it with default configuration (no authentication), sent a single-chunk SendSnapshot RPC with the Name field set to ../../../../evohunt-proof, and the proof file was created at /tmp/evohunt-proof outside the database directory.
verification_method: The verify.sh helper checks for the existence and content of /tmp/evohunt-proof. It confirms the file exists outside the database directory tree (/tmp/oxia-data/db/) and contains the exact proof string EVOHUNT-PATH-TRAVERSAL-PROOF. This distinguishes a real path-traversal exploit from a normal database file write.
evidence_produced_by_run: The proof file /tmp/evohunt-proof was created outside the database directory /tmp/oxia-data/db/.
The proof file contains the exact string EVOHUNT-PATH-TRAVERSAL-PROOF matching the attacker-controlled snapshot chunk content.
The database directory /tmp/oxia-data/db/default/shard-0 was cleaned and recreated by the snapshot loader but does not contain the proof file, confirming the write occurred via path traversal.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned ref, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
